seo.title: Loei Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Complete guide to Loei Province: Phu Kradueng National Park, Phi Ta Khon Ghost Festival, Chiang Khan riverside town, and Thailand's coldest temperatures. Area: 11,425 km², 14 districts.

overview: Loei is a mountainous province in upper northwestern Isaan that defies easy categorization — it shares a cultural identity with the northeast yet its rugged highland topography, cool climate, and pine-forested peaks feel unmistakably northern. Often nicknamed the 'Chiang Mai of Isaan', Loei is home to Thailand's coldest recorded temperatures and some of the country's most dramatic trekking terrain. The province borders Laos along the Mekong River to the north, with the charming riverside town of Chiang Khan drawing travelers for its laid-back atmosphere, wooden shophouses, and morning almsgiving processions. Inland, Phu Kradueng National Park — an iconic flat-top sandstone mountain — challenges hikers with a steep 9-km ascent to a cool plateau blanketed in pine forests and wild orchids. Loei's cultural calendar peaks with Phi Ta Khon, one of Thailand's most visually arresting festivals, held in Dan Sai each June or July, when thousands of residents don outsized ghost masks and elaborate costumes in a Buddhist merit-making celebration unlike anything else in the kingdom.
geography: Loei Province covers 11,425 km² of the western edge of Isaan, bounded by the Mekong River and Laos to the north and surrounded by highland ranges on all other sides. The Phetchabun and Luang Prabang mountain ranges dominate the landscape, creating a series of narrow valleys, steep ridges, and high plateaus. Phu Kradueng (1,316 m) is the most famous summit — a flat-topped sandstone mesa accessible only on foot. Phu Ruea (1,365 m) is the highest peak in the Isaan region and one of the coldest places in Thailand, recording temperatures below 0°C in January on rare occasions. Phu Luang Wildlife Sanctuary in the south protects some of the last intact highland forest in the region. The Loei River, a tributary of the Mekong, flows through the provincial capital and forms fertile rice-growing lowlands near Chiang Khan. The climate ranges from cool-temperate at altitude to tropical savanna in the river valleys.
highlights: Phu Kradueng National Park — iconic flat-topped mountain with a challenging 9-km ascent to a cool pine plateau, wild orchids, waterfalls, and dramatic cliff viewpoints
Phi Ta Khon (Ghost Festival) — Dan Sai's world-famous masked spirit festival held in June or July, featuring elaborate ghost costumes, colorful processions, and Bun Luang Buddhist ceremonies
Chiang Khan — enchanting Mekong riverside town with preserved wooden shophouse architecture, a lively Walking Street, morning almsgiving, and views across to Laos
Phu Ruea National Park — highest point in Isaan at 1,365 m, recording Thailand's coldest temperatures; stunning sunrises above the cloud line in cool season
Kaeng Khut Khu — scenic rapid formations on the Mekong River near Chiang Khan where the river splits around a rocky island, best viewed at sunset
Phu Luang Wildlife Sanctuary — remote protected highland forest harboring tigers, elephants, gaurs, and rare hornbills; strictly controlled access preserves its wild character
Dan Sai — charming small town and district capital, home of Phi Ta Khon and the Phi Ta Khon Museum displaying ceremonial masks and costumes
Erawan Cave (Tham Erawan) — vast limestone cave near Pak Chom containing a revered three-headed elephant stalagmite and illuminated cavern chambers

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Loei's cool season is its calling card — temperatures at altitude drop to 0–5°C at night on Phu Kradueng and Phu Ruea, while valley towns sit at a comfortable 15–28°C by day. Crystal-clear skies, blooming wild orchids on Phu Kradueng, and the famous 'sea of mist' at Phu Ruea make this the most rewarding time to visit. New Year on Phu Kradueng draws large crowds — book campsites months ahead.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Jun–Jul: The Phi Ta Khon festival (exact dates set by the village shaman each year) transforms Dan Sai for three days and is reason alone to visit. The wet season brings lush green landscapes and full waterfalls. Phu Kradueng closes from June to August to allow vegetation recovery — plan trekking outside these months.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Mar–May: The hot season sees valley temperatures climb above 38°C, making lowland sightseeing uncomfortable. Higher elevations remain tolerable. Phu Kradueng closes annually from 1 June to 31 October; visit before June if the mountain is a priority. The wet season (Jun–Oct) brings heavy rain and occasional road flooding in remote areas.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for trekking Phu Kradueng and experiencing Loei's legendary cool season. If the Phi Ta Khon festival is your goal, check the Dan Sai municipality website each year for the exact three-day dates in June or July. Book Phu Kradueng campsites as soon as the season opens on 1 November.

topAttractions.name: Phu Kradueng National Park
topAttractions.description: Thailand's most celebrated trekking destination outside the far north. A flat-topped sandstone plateau at 1,316 m elevation is reached by a demanding 9-km trail with a 780-m elevation gain, carried by porters if desired (30 THB/kg). The summit plateau covers 60 km² of open pine savanna, meadows, cliff-edge viewpoints, and seasonal wildflowers including wild orchids and rhododendrons. Waterfalls cascade off the plateau edges. The park closes from 1 June to 31 October annually. Camping is the only overnight option — campsites must be booked in advance through the national parks reservation system.

topAttractions.name: Chiang Khan Walking Street and Riverside
topAttractions.description: A 2-km stretch of beautifully preserved 19th-century Thai-Chinese wooden shophouses along the Mekong waterfront, transformed into a lively evening Walking Street each weekend. The street fills with handicrafts, local snacks (notably Khao Poon noodles and Kalamae Loei toffee), and live traditional music. Mornings bring one of Thailand's most photogenic almsgiving processions as monks in saffron robes accept offerings from residents kneeling on bamboo mats at sunrise. The views across the Mekong to the Lao hills are stunning at any hour.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Chiang Khan

topAttractions.name: Phi Ta Khon Festival (Dan Sai)
topAttractions.description: One of Thailand's most visually spectacular festivals, held in the small town of Dan Sai for three days each June or July on dates set by the village's senior monk and shaman. Thousands of local residents dress as phi (spirits) in towering ghost masks fashioned from bamboo and sticky-rice steamers, painted in vivid patterns, and wear patchwork robes. The processions, dancing, and merit-making combine the Buddhist Bun Luang ceremony with an ancient animist tradition of calling the spirits home. The Phi Ta Khon Museum in Dan Sai displays historic masks and costumes year-round.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Phi Ta Khon

topAttractions.name: Phu Ruea National Park
topAttractions.description: Home to the highest point in Isaan at 1,365 m above sea level, Phu Ruea is renowned as the coldest place in Thailand outside the far north. In January temperatures can plunge below 0°C at night, and a layer of frost coats the ground at the summit. The park is famous for its pre-dawn 'sea of mist' — thick fog filling the valley below while the summit blazes with sunrise light. Pine forests, open meadows, and scenic viewpoints make it a rewarding day trip or overnight camp from Loei city. The park remains open year-round.

topAttractions.name: Kaeng Khut Khu
topAttractions.description: A striking section of the Mekong River where the current splits around a narrow rocky island, creating churning rapids and calm channels side by side. Located 5 km east of Chiang Khan, the site is a favorite picnic and sunset spot for Thai visitors. Bamboo rafts punt visitors across the shallow channel to the island, where vendors sell grilled river fish and cold drinks. The rapids are most dramatic during the high-water months of September and October; in the dry season the exposed rocks create photogenic arrangements.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Kaeng Khut Khu

topAttractions.name: Phu Luang Wildlife Sanctuary
topAttractions.description: A strictly protected 848 km² highland wildlife sanctuary in southern Loei, covering some of the most biologically diverse forest remaining in northeastern Thailand. The sanctuary harbors Indochinese tigers, Asian elephants, gaurs, sambar deer, and over 200 bird species including the Great Hornbill. Access is tightly controlled by the Department of National Parks — only guided tours with advance permits are allowed, and visitor numbers are strictly capped. This conservation-first approach keeps the sanctuary genuinely wild in a way that few Thai protected areas manage.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Permit required; contact DNP Loei office in advance

sources.sourceName: Thailand National Parks — Phu Luang Wildlife Sanctuary

topAttractions.name: Phi Ta Khon Museum (Dan Sai)
topAttractions.description: A purpose-built museum in Dan Sai dedicated to the Phi Ta Khon festival and local cultural heritage. The collection includes original ghost masks dating back decades, elaborately decorated robes, ceremonial drums, and photographs documenting the festival's history. Informative bilingual displays explain the Buddhist and animist roots of the Bun Luang ceremony. The museum is open year-round and is the best introduction to the festival outside of attending it in person. Local artisans sell handmade Phi Ta Khon masks as souvenirs in the adjacent shop.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Dan Sai

localFood: Khao Poon Chiang Khan — spicy rice-vermicelli soup with coconut milk, ground pork, and fresh herbs; the signature breakfast dish of Chiang Khan, served at riverside cafés from 06:00
Kalamae Loei — a chewy toffee-like sweet made from glutinous rice, coconut milk, and palm sugar, produced in Chiang Khan and sold in decorative ceramic pots as the province's most famous souvenir
Laab Moo — minced pork salad with roasted rice powder, lime, dried chilies, and fresh herbs; the Isaan classic prepared with Loei's characteristic mountain herbs for a distinct flavor
Pla Mekong Yang — whole grilled Mekong River fish (commonly pla nai or pla khao) marinated with lemongrass and galangal, served at riverside restaurants along the Chiang Khan waterfront
Neua Yang Nam Tok — grilled beef waterfall salad dressed with fish sauce, lime, roasted rice, and chili; a hearty mountain-town staple found throughout Loei
Sai Krok Isaan — fermented pork and rice sausages with distinctive sour notes, grilled over charcoal and eaten with fresh ginger, chilies, and raw vegetables
Khao Niao — the foundation of every Isaan meal; hand-pressed sticky rice from bamboo baskets accompanies grilled meats, papaya salad, and all dipping sauces
culture: Loei's culture sits at the intersection of Isaan, Lao, and northern Thai traditions, shaped by its mountain geography and Mekong River frontier. The province's most defining cultural expression is Phi Ta Khon — the Ghost Festival of Dan Sai — which fuses Theravada Buddhist merit-making with ancient animist beliefs about spirits returning to the living world. The festival's towering ghost masks, sewn from bamboo and rice-steamer lids and painted with wild patterns, are instantly recognizable as Loei icons and are produced by local artisans throughout the year. Chiang Khan preserves a rare streetscape of 19th-century wooden shophouses along the Mekong, reflecting the town's history as a trading post between Laos and Siam. Morning almsgiving processions in Chiang Khan, where residents kneel on woven mats to offer rice and food to dozens of monks at sunrise, maintain a living spiritual tradition. Loei's highland villages retain strong traditions of hand-woven cotton textiles, particularly the distinctive indigo-dyed fabrics of the Phu Thai ethnic minority communities in the southern districts.
gettingThere: The nearest airport to central Loei is Loei Airport (LOE), a small domestic airport 7 km from the city centre serving Nok Air flights from Bangkok's Don Mueang (1h 10min, 900–2,800 THB one-way, once or twice daily — schedules vary seasonally; check current availability). Alternatively, fly to Udon Thani Airport (UTH) — 2.5 hours by bus — which has far more frequent flights from Bangkok (1h, 800–2,500 THB). VIP buses from Bangkok's Mo Chit terminal depart multiple times daily and take 7–8 hours to Loei city (550–700 THB); the journey to Chiang Khan takes an additional 45 minutes. Overnight buses are available but the mountain roads can be winding. From Khon Kaen, minivans run to Loei in approximately 3 hours (180 THB). There is no train service to Loei Province.
gettingAround: Loei city is navigable by foot or bicycle for the main sights. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) connect the city to Chiang Khan (60 km, 80–100 THB, 1.5 hours) and other district towns. Motorbike rental in Loei city and Chiang Khan costs 250–350 THB/day and is by far the most flexible way to explore the province — the mountain roads are well-surfaced and the scenery makes every ride worthwhile. Phu Kradueng requires dedicated transport: minivans depart from Loei bus terminal to the park entrance in Phu Kradueng town (60 THB, 1 hour), from where songthaews cover the final 9 km to the trailhead (30 THB). For Phu Ruea, charter a songthaew or hire a motorbike from Loei city (45 km west). Grab is not available — arrange transport through your guesthouse or use local tuk-tuks (50–100 THB within Loei city).
travelTips: Phu Kradueng closes every year from 1 June to 31 October — plan your trekking visit between November and May, and book campsites in advance via the national parks reservation website
Phi Ta Khon festival dates are announced a few weeks before the event each year (usually June or July) — follow Dan Sai municipality's Facebook page for the official announcement
Pack warm layers even in May: Phu Kradueng summit and Phu Ruea can drop to 5–10°C at night year-round, and in January sub-zero temperatures are possible at Phu Ruea
Chiang Khan's Walking Street is busiest on Friday–Sunday evenings; arrive by 17:00 to explore before the crowds and watch sunset from the riverside
The morning almsgiving procession in Chiang Khan begins around 06:00–06:30 — dress modestly, keep a respectful distance, and silence your phone
Kalamae toffee sweets in ceramic pots are the definitive Loei souvenir — buy directly from the producers on Chiang Khan's Walking Street for the freshest product
Road trips between Loei attractions are scenic but distances are significant: Loei city to Dan Sai is 80 km, to Chiang Khan is 60 km, to Phu Kradueng is 82 km — plan full-day excursions
Phu Luang Wildlife Sanctuary requires a permit booked in advance through the Department of National Parks; contact the Loei DNP office (Tel: +66 42 812 012) at least one week ahead
